10.1016/j.jmp.2004.11.002
The Bradley–Terry–Luce model (BTL model) for paired comparisons assumes that for any element  
                         a 
                         ∈ 
                         A 
                       there exists a positive real number  
                         φ 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         ) 
                       such that for all elements  
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         A 
                      , 
                         
                            (1) 
                            
                               p 
                               ( 
                               a 
                               , 
                               b 
                               ) 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     φ 
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  
                                     φ 
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     ) 
                                     + 
                                     φ 
                                     ( 
                                     b 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      ( Bradley & Terry, 1952 ;  Luce, 1959 ). Usually, the real number  
                         p 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       is interpreted as the probability that  a  is chosen when  
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       is presented. The order of stimuli may express the fact that in a paired comparison experiment stimulus  a  is presented first, followed by stimulus  b .

It is a well-known fact that the BTL model can be derived from axioms about binary choice probabilities

                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                      : Let  
                         p 
                         : 
                         A 
                         × 
                         A 
                         → 
                         ] 
                         0 
                         , 
                         1 
                         [ 
                       be a function on the cartesian product of a nonempty set  A . Then the following axiom system is necessary and sufficient for Eq. ( 1 ) to hold ( Luce, 1959 ;  Suppes & Zinnes, 1963 ):  Balance Condition : For  
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         A 
                      ,  
                         p 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                         + 
                         p 
                         ( 
                         b 
                         , 
                         a 
                         ) 
                         = 
                         1 
                      .  Product Rule : For any triple  
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         , 
                         c 
                         ∈ 
                         A 
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                     - 
                                     p 
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                               · 
                               
                                  
                                     p

                                     b 
                                     , 
                                     c 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                     - 
                                     p 
                                     ( 
                                     b 
                                     , 
                                     c 
                                     )

                                     p

                                     a 
                                     )

                                     - 
                                     p 
                                     ( 
                                     c 
                                     , 
                                     a 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               1 
                               .

The first extension of the BTL model to account for the order of presentation dates back to 1975:  Beaver and Gokhale (1975)  proposed an additive order effect: For the ordered pair  
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                      , the preference probability  
                         p 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       is given by 
                         
                            (2) 
                            
                               p 
                               ( 
                               a 
                               , 
                               b 
                               ) 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     φ 
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     ) 
                                     + 
                                     ε 
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  
                                     φ 
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     ) 
                                     + 
                                     φ 
                                     ( 
                                     b 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                      Moreover, they assume that the order effect parameters  
                         ε 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                         ∈ 
                         [ 
                         - 
                         φ 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         ) 
                         , 
                         φ 
                         ( 
                         b 
                         ) 
                         ] 
                       depend only on the object pair, that is, for all  
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         A 
                      ,  
                         ε 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                         = 
                         ε 
                         ( 
                         b 
                         , 
                         a 
                         ) 
                      . As an alternative, a multiplicative order effect has been proposed by  Davidson and Beaver (1977) : 
                         
                            (3) 
                            
                               p 
                               ( 
                               a 
                               , 
                               b 
                               ) 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     φ 
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  
                                     φ 
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     ) 
                                     + 
                                     ε 
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                     ) 
                                     φ 
                                     ( 
                                     b 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                      In order to reduce the number of parameters in the model,  Davidson and Beaver (1977)  adopt the assumption that  
                         ε 
                       is a symmetric function of  
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                      , i.e., for all  
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         A 
                      ,  
                         ε 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                         = 
                         ε 
                         ( 
                         b 
                         , 
                         a 
                         ) 
                      . Obviously, the case where  
                         ε 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       is a constant function of  
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       is of special interest because of the reduced number of parameters required to specify the model. The usefulness of this biased version of the BTL model has been shown in different studies (e.g.,  Koehler & Ridpath, 1982 ;  Lukas, 1991 ).

In psychophysics, choice probabilities are often used in a slightly different way. For instance, to determine a discrimination threshold for a reference stimulus  b , it is common practice in psychophysics to fit a distribution function

                         ) 
                       which can be estimated from the empirical data. Typically,

                      Once we have chosen the functional form of the psychometric function, the further procedure for modeling empirical data is straightforward (cf.  Wichman & Hill, 2001 ): First, the best fitting parameters

Already  Luce (1959)  pointed out that there is a simple relationship between the BTL model and the logistic curve: If we define

                      (cf.  Falmagne, 1985; Luce, 1959 ). In particular, a Thurstone model with independent discriminal processes results for which all differences between discriminal processes are logistically distributed random variables. However, whereas the relationship between  Luce's (1959)  choice axiom and  Thurstone's (1927)  law of comparative judgement has been thoroughly investigated (cf.  Yellott, 1977 ), it is still an open question how psychophysical threshold models are related to probabilistic choice models. The present paper gives a partial answer by introducing a straightforward (and as far as I know: new) extension of the BTL model that can be derived from Eq. ( 4 ): Let us assume that Eq. The paper is organized as follows:  Section 2  provides different representation and uniqueness theorems for the extended BTL model. First of all,  Lemma 2  presents a modification of the product rule that is necessary and sufficient for Eq. ( 5 ) to hold. As a consequence, a characterization of the BTL model by  Davidson and Beaver (1977)  results (cf.  Corollary 3 ). A severe drawback of the extended product rule of  Lemma 2  is that it cannot be put to an empirical test easily. Therefore,  Theorem 4  provides a different characterization of the extended BTL model that is based exclusively on choice probabilities  
                         p 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                      .  Theorem 5  shows that the extended BTL model becomes the BTL model if the balance condition  
                         p 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                         + 
                         p 
                         ( 
                         b 
                         , 
                         a 
                         ) 
                         = 
                         1 
                       is satisfied for all  
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         A 
                      . The uniqueness problem is addressed in  Theorem 6 : Different from the ordinary BTL model, the homomorphism  
                         φ 
                       of the extended BTL model is only a log-interval scale. Moreover, the order effect parameter  
                         ρ 
                         ( 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       is uniquely determined, and the exponent  
                         ψ 
                         ( 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       is measured by means of a ratio scale  
                         ψ 
                      .  Section 3  is on the logistic threshold model ( 6 ):  Theorem 7  clarifies the relationship between the extended BTL model and the logistic threshold model.  Theorem 8  provides a simple characterization of the logistic threshold model which is based on a fairly simple functional equation between choice probabilities. Finally,  Section 4  addresses open problems and points to further research.

In the following, different representation and uniqueness theorems for the extended BTL model are presented. To begin with, a simple modification of the product rule is deduced which is necessary and sufficient for Eq. ( 5 ) to hold:

Immediate consequence of  Lemma 2 . □

The extended product rule of  Lemma 2  has severe drawbacks: In order to apply Eq. ( 7 ) to empirical data we have to specify the parameters  
                         ψ 
                         ( 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       and  
                         ψ 
                         ( 
                         c 
                         ) 
                      . However, in general, these values are unknown. As a consequence, Eq. ( 7 ) cannot be put to an empirical test easily. Therefore,  Theorem 4  provides a different characterization that is based exclusively on choice probabilities:

The proof of  Theorem 4  indicates that the extended BTL model coincides with the ordinary BTL model if the balance condition  
                         p 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                         + 
                         p 
                         ( 
                         b 
                         , 
                         a 
                         ) 
                         = 
                         1 
                       is satisfied for all  
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         A 
                      .  Theorem 5  analyzes the relationship between both models for the special case of a countable set  A  of objects.  
                         Theorem 5 
                         
                            Let us assume an extended BTL model  
                            
                               〈 
                               A 
                               , 
                               p 
                               〉 
                             
                            that satisfies the balance condition  
                            
                               p 
                               ( 
                               a 
                               , 
                               b 
                               ) 
                               + 
                               p 
                               ( 
                               b 
                               , 
                               a 
                               ) 
                               = 
                               1 
                            .  Moreover ,  let us assume that there exists a sequence  
                            
                               { 
                               
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               : 
                               i 
                               ∈ 
                               N 
                               }

                                  1. 
                                  
                                     Either  
                                     
                                        N 
                                        = 
                                        N 
                                     ,  or there exists an integer  
                                     
                                        n 
                                        ∈ 
                                        N

                                        = 
                                        { 
                                        1

                                        … 
                                        , 
                                        n 
                                        }

                                        a 
                                        ∈ 
                                        A

                                        i 
                                        ∈ 
                                        N 
                                      
                                     such that  
                                     
                                        a 
                                        = 
                                        
                                           
                                              a 
                                           
                                           
                                              i 
                                           
                                        
                                     ; 
                               
                               
                                  3. 
                                  
                                     If  
                                     
                                        i 
                                        , 
                                        i 
                                        + 
                                        1 
                                        ∈ 
                                        N

                                        p

                                              i

                                              a

                               φ 
                               ( 
                               a

In particular, the proof of  Theorem 6  can be adapted to Eqs. ( 8 ) and ( 9 ) (cf. the BTL model by  Davidson & Beaver, 1977 ): For both models,  
                         φ 
                       is a ratio scale and  
                         ρ 
                       is uniquely determined.

Finally, the relevance of the extended BTL model for psychophysical research should be emphasized: Basically, two different approaches for estimating thresholds are distinguished in psychophysics which are usually denoted as  parametric  versus  nonparametric  methods (cf.  Klein, 2001 ). Typically, in parametric methods, the functional shape of the psychometric function is fixed and the data are used to estimate the parameters of the underlying probability distribution function (cf.  Section 1 ). Whereas the logistic threshold model provides a common example of a parametric model, the extended BTL model can be attributed to the nonparametric approach since Eq. ( 5 ) does not determine the functional form of the psychometric function  
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               b 
                            
                         
                      . Therefore, the extended BTL model can provide a useful tool to extract information from psychometric data, especially if we only have little prior knowledge of the exact shape of  
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               b 
                            
                         
                      : If, for instance, the homomorphism  
                         φ 
                         : 
                         A 
                         → 
                         ] 
                         0 
                         , 
                         ∞ 
                         [ 
                       in Eq. ( 5 ) is a power function of the form  
                         
                            
                               φ 
                               ( 
                               a 
                               ) 
                               = 
                               α 
                               
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                                  
                                     β 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                            
                         
                      where  
                         α 
                         > 
                         0 
                       and  
                         β 
                         ∈ 
                         R 
                       are fixed constants, then the psychometric function is given by the equation 
                         
                            (12) 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     b 
                                  
                               
                               ( 
                               a

                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     1

                               .

It has been shown in  Section 1  that a logistic threshold model is also an extended BTL model. Now the relationship between both models is specified:  Theorem 7  shows that the logistic threshold model is merely an extended BTL model with a specific representation

The importance of  Theorem 8  is quite obvious: An empirical evaluation of Axioms ( 13 )–( 15 ) can replace a global goodness of fit test for the logistic probability distribution. Despite the fact that testing the axioms of a probabilistic measurement system is highly problematic 
                         4 
                      
                      
                         4 
                         This does not mean that it is impossible to test the axioms of a probabilistic measurement model. For instance,  Hamerle and Tutz (1980)  have developed general goodness-of-fit tests for probabilistic response models. Similarly, Iverson and  Falmagne (1985)  have worked out a likelihood ratio method. Maybe, these approaches can be adapted to the axioms of the present paper. 
                       (cf.  Bossuyt & Roskam, 1989 ;  Hamerle & Tutz, 1980 ;  Iverson & Falmagne, 1985 ), the present approach has considerable advantages I want to highlight briefly. If a quantitative model such as the logistic threshold model fails to give a good overall fit to a set of data, little information is provided about how to modify the theory. In contrast to this, the present theory takes the form of a set of axioms. Testing these axioms separately may reveal deficiencies of the model. This knowledge provides a basis for modifying the axiom system. Moreover, a further advantage of the axiomatic approach as compared to goodness of fit tests is that the maximum likelihood estimation of parameters is dispensable. The axioms of the measurement system can be tested directly without applying numerical methods.

Lemma 2  provides one of the main results of the present paper. It shows that the extended BTL model can be derived from a simple modification of the product rule. Moreover, the uniqueness theorem shows that Eq. ( 5 ) is a useful generalization of the ordinary BTL model (cf.  Theorem 6 ). However, in order to compare the extended BTL model with Eqs. ( 2 ) and ( 3 ), similar representation and uniqueness theorems have to be deduced for the BTL models by  Beaver and Gokhale (1975)  and  Davidson and Beaver (1977) . Although each model is a straightforward extension of the ordinary BTL model, there are no corresponding extensions of the product rule. A forthcoming paper should fill this gap by providing representation and uniqueness theorems.

Section 3  presents an axiomatic approach to specify the shape of the psychometric function that is highly influenced by the theory of derived measurement: The logistic shape of the psychometric function was derived from simple axioms about binary choice probabilities (Theorem  8 ). In particular, the relationship between the logistic probability distribution and the extended BTL model has been worked out (Theorem  7 ). However, from a psychophysical point of view, the restriction to Eq. ( 6 ) is by no means justified theoretically. Therefore, the logistic curve should be replaced by other probability distribution functions. The following characterization of Eq. ( 12 ) provides a very simple example of such a modification:
